title: Docker Resource Usage

services.name: fintech-kafka
services.notes: CPU spikes correlated with transaction bursts; healthiest memory usage

services.name: fintech-ml-engine
services.notes: Largest memory consumer per-service; Python process with loaded XGBoost model. High block I/O from reading model files.

services.name: fintech-zookeeper
services.notes: Java process; uses 180MB baseline even at idle. JVM heap overhead.

services.name: fintech-postgres
services.notes: Very efficient for ~12K transactions. 13GB of data written (transactions + predictions).

services.name: fintech-ingestion-gateway
services.notes: Most network I/O of all services. Go binary ~45MB RSS is very efficient.

services.name: fintech-redis
services.notes: Minimal resource usage. AOF persistence causing block I/O.

services.name: fintech-dashboard
services.notes: Static file server (Nginx). Only serves built assets. Zero CPU idle.

services.name: fintech-simulator
services.notes: Tiny Go binary. At 10 TPS currently. Negligible footprint.

services.name: fintech-nginx
services.notes: Minimal resource usage. Idle because dashboard/gateway are accessed directly by benchmark.

findings: Total system footprint: ~940 MB RAM, ~37% CPU — well within typical production host limits
Kafka is the most resource-intensive service with CPU spikes up to 140%, but averages ~33%
ML Engine and Zookeeper are tied for highest steady-state RAM usage (~180MB each)
Go services (Gateway, Simulator) are extremely memory-efficient at 8-45MB each
PostgreSQL is very lean at 55MB for 12K+ rows — the SQLite of OLTP databases
Redis at 12MB is negligible — AOF persistence overhead is minor
Dashboard (Nginx SPA) has zero CPU and 15MB memory — static file serving is free
The entire stack fits comfortably in under 1GB RAM
